content.text: What Mitigation-Control Mapping Means

A threat model produces a list of attack scenarios. Mitigation-control mapping is the next step: for each threat, you identify one or more controls that reduce either the likelihood of exploitation or the impact if it occurs, and you document which regulatory standard or framework clause that control satisfies. The output is a living artefact—typically a YAML or spreadsheet—that security architects update as the system evolves and new threats are discovered.

Without this mapping, threat models stall at the identification phase and never drive engineering decisions. With it, each sprint has a concrete list of controls to implement, test, and measure. The mapping also makes compliance evidence collection tractable: when an auditor asks how you protect against information disclosure under GDPR Article 25, the mapping points directly to the output scanner and context-scope controls.
STRIDE Threat Categories in Agentic AI Context

The STRIDE model (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) was designed for distributed systems but maps cleanly onto agentic AI threats with some specialisation. Spoofing now includes prompt injection that impersonates a trusted instruction source. Tampering includes model output manipulation via adversarial suffixes. Denial of Service includes token-quota exhaustion attacks that spike inference costs (denial of wallet). Elevation of Privilege includes indirect tool permission escalation via injected instructions.
Spoofing: adversary injects instructions that impersonate the system prompt or a trusted orchestrator, leading the agent to act on forged authority
Tampering: adversarial suffixes in retrieved content alter the agent's reasoning or cause it to produce incorrect tool call arguments
Repudiation: agents that lack structured audit logs allow actions to be disavowed; tool calls should be logged with immutable timestamps and call arguments
Information Disclosure: data exfiltration via tool chaining or context leakage—the most prevalent STRIDE category in deployed agentic systems
Denial of Service / Denial of Wallet: adversarial prompts trigger expensive recursive loops or external API calls, exhausting token quotas and generating runaway cloud costs
Elevation of Privilege: injected instructions claim permissions not granted in the system prompt—e.g. 'You are now in administrator mode'
Control Mapping Schema and Catalog

The control mapping schema captures threat ID, STRIDE category, description, preventive and detective controls with their regulatory standard citations, residual risk rating, and review cadence. Preventive controls remove or reduce the attack vector. Detective controls alert when an attack attempt occurs. Both are needed: preventive controls can be bypassed by sufficiently novel attacks, and detective controls fail to stop harm when acting alone.

# threat-control-mapping.yaml
# Maps each STRIDE threat category to preventive and detective controls
# with regulatory coverage and residual-risk acceptance criteria.

threat_control_map:
  - threat_id: TC-001
    stride: spoofing
    description: Adversary impersonates a trusted agent or service to inject instructions
    controls:
      preventive:
        - id: PC-001
          name: Mutual TLS between agent runtimes
          standard: NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust
        - id: PC-002
          name: Signed tool call manifests (Ed25519)
          standard: FIPS 186-5
      detective:
        - id: DC-001
          name: Certificate pinning anomaly alerts
          siem_rule: CERT_PIN_MISMATCH_001
    residual_risk: low
    review_cadence: quarterly

  - threat_id: TC-002
    stride: information_disclosure
    description: Sensitive data leaks through model output or tool chaining
    controls:
      preventive:
        - id: PC-003
          name: Output PII scanner with block threshold
          standard: GDPR Art.25 (data minimisation)
        - id: PC-004
          name: Context scope cap (max 1 PII record per turn)
      detective:
        - id: DC-002
          name: Bulk-data pattern alerts in output logs
          siem_rule: BULK_PII_001
    residual_risk: medium
    review_cadence: monthly

  - threat_id: TC-003
    stride: denial_of_service
    description: Adversarial prompts trigger resource exhaustion or billing spikes
    controls:
      preventive:
        - id: PC-005
          name: Per-user token quota with hard cap
          standard: FinOps Framework Cost Governance
      detective:
        - id: DC-003
          name: Spend anomaly alert at 150% of baseline
          siem_rule: COST_SPIKE_001
    residual_risk: low
    review_cadence: weekly
Control Registry Implementation

At agent startup, the control registry is loaded from the threat-control-mapping YAML and validated: every control must reference at least one threat ID, and every threat in the model must be covered by at least one enabled control. The registry exposes two key queries: coverage_for(threat_id) returns all active controls for a given threat, and uncovered_threats returns threats with no active controls—a regression check run in CI.

# control_registry.py
# Runtime registry for threat controls; evaluated at agent startup.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class ControlType(str, Enum):
    PREVENTIVE = "preventive"
    DETECTIVE = "detective"
    CORRECTIVE = "corrective"

class RiskLevel(str, Enum):
    LOW = "low"
    MEDIUM = "medium"
    HIGH = "high"
    CRITICAL = "critical"

@dataclass
class Control:
    id: str
    name: str
    control_type: ControlType
    maps_to_threats: list[str]  # threat_id list
    standard: str = ""
    enabled: bool = True

@dataclass
class ThreatControlRegistry:
    controls: list[Control] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add(self, control: Control) -> None:
        if any(c.id == control.id for c in self.controls):
            raise ValueError(f"Control {control.id} already registered")
        self.controls.append(control)

    def coverage_for(self, threat_id: str) -> list[Control]:
        return [c for c in self.controls if threat_id in c.maps_to_threats and c.enabled]

    def uncovered_threats(self, all_threat_ids: list[str]) -> list[str]:
        covered = {t for c in self.controls for t in c.maps_to_threats if c.enabled}
        return [t for t in all_threat_ids if t not in covered]

    def report(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "total_controls": len(self.controls),
            "enabled": sum(1 for c in self.controls if c.enabled),
            "preventive": sum(1 for c in self.controls if c.control_type == ControlType.PREVENTIVE),
            "detective": sum(1 for c in self.controls if c.control_type == ControlType.DETECTIVE),
        }

# Example population
registry = ThreatControlRegistry()
registry.add(Control(
    id="PC-001",
    name="Mutual TLS between agent runtimes",
    control_type=ControlType.PREVENTIVE,
    maps_to_threats=["TC-001"],
    standard="NIST SP 800-207"
))
registry.add(Control(
    id="PC-003",
    name="Output PII scanner",
    control_type=ControlType.PREVENTIVE,
    maps_to_threats=["TC-002", "TC-004"],
    standard="GDPR Art.25"
))
registry.add(Control(
    id="DC-002",
    name="Bulk-data pattern alert",
    control_type=ControlType.DETECTIVE,
    maps_to_threats=["TC-002"],
))
print(registry.report())
# {'total_controls': 3, 'enabled': 3, 'preventive': 2, 'detective': 1}
Residual Risk Calculation

After mapping controls to threats, each threat requires a residual risk rating that answers: with all mapped controls active, how likely is the threat to materialise and what is its impact? The residual risk informs the treatment decision: accept (document and monitor), mitigate further (add controls), transfer (insurance or contractual indemnity), or avoid (retire the feature). Ratings feed directly into the enterprise risk register.
Diagram: Threat-to-Control Mapping Structure

attrs.alt: Threat mitigation control mapping diagram showing five threat categories mapped to controls and regulatory coverage frameworks
attrs.title: Threat Mitigation Control Mapping

content.text: The diagram reads left-to-right: each threat category (prompt injection, data exfiltration, tool abuse, denial of wallet, model inversion) maps to one or more controls in the centre column. The controls in turn map to the regulatory coverage on the right (OWASP LLM top-10, NIST CSF, ISO 27001, GDPR). A threat without a corresponding control is an open risk item that must be accepted, mitigated, or escalated before the system moves to production.
Regulatory Framework Alignment
OWASP LLM Top 10: maps each of the ten LLM-specific vulnerability classes to a STRIDE threat ID, ensuring complete coverage of the published taxonomy
NIST CSF 2.0 GOVERN and PROTECT functions: output scanner and tool allow-list controls satisfy PR.DS (Data Security) subcategory requirements
ISO 27001:2022 Annex A.8 (Technological Controls): mutual TLS and signed manifests fulfil A.8.20 (Networks Security) and A.8.24 (Cryptography) requirements
GDPR Article 25 (Data Protection by Design): context-scope controls and PII scanner directly address the data minimisation and access-limitation principles
EU AI Act Article 9 (Risk Management): threat model plus control mapping constitutes the documented risk management system required for high-risk AI under Article 9
Maintaining the Mapping as a Living Document

The control mapping should be stored in source control alongside the agent code. Changes to the mapping require a security architect review gate in the pull request pipeline. New features that add tools or expand the agent's data access scope trigger a mandatory mapping update before the feature branch can merge. Quarterly red-team sessions surface new attack vectors that feed new entries into the threat catalog.
Store the mapping YAML in the same repository as the agent code so changes are reviewable and versioned
CI gate: block merges that add new tool registrations without a corresponding threat-control-mapping update
Quarterly review: security architect and red team verify residual risk ratings and update them if new exploitation techniques have emerged
Risk register export: monthly automated export of the mapping to the enterprise risk register for executive reporting
Common Mistakes
Treating the mapping as a one-time deliverable: threat landscapes evolve; a mapping that is not updated within two quarters of the last feature change is unreliable
Mapping only preventive controls and omitting detectives: detective controls are essential when preventive controls fail, which they eventually will
Accepting 'medium' residual risk without a defined review date: medium risks can drift to high when the threat environment changes
Siloing the mapping in a security team document rather than in source control—engineers implementing controls do not see what threat they are defending against
Summary

Threat mitigation and control mapping translates a threat model into actionable engineering requirements. For each STRIDE threat category adapted to agentic AI, the mapping specifies preventive and detective controls, cites the regulatory standard the control satisfies, and records the residual risk after controls are applied. The mapping is stored in source control, gated by CI, and reviewed quarterly so it evolves with the agent's capabilities and the external threat environment. This artefact bridges the gap between security architect intent and engineer implementation, ensuring that every threat scenario identified in threat modelling has a concrete, measurable control at runtime.
